464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding**: no documented first-touch-to-first-class flow (intake form → induction → intro class → check-in cadence)
- **Churn/cancellation**: no exit interview process, win-back triggers, or pause protocol when members disengage
- **Member check-ins**: no systematic retention touchpoint schedule (30/60/90 day, post-class, milestone-based)
- **Coaching development**: no roadmap for progression, skill assessment, or performance feedback loops
- **Class quality audit**: no standardised observation rubric or accountability mechanism for coaching consistency
- **Referral programme**: no mechanics for tracking, incentives, or member activation around referrals
- **Milestone/outcomes tracking**: no process to capture transformation data, celebrate wins, or use them in marketing/retention
- **Data privacy & GDPR**: no documented handling of member data, consent capture, or retention/deletion policies
- **Order supplies & sundries**: no inventory, vendor, or reorder trigger process (cleaning, towels, equipment)
- **Troubleshooting guide**: no escalation path for common member complaints (access issues, billing, class scheduling conflicts)

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Coaches SOPs (20/24)**: likely missing coaching standards (cue consistency, form correction protocol, class pacing); probably no template for session planning or injury/red-flag response
- **Clients SOPs (23/33)**: heavy gap between general client processes and *retention mechanics*; no systematic win-back or re-engagement rules
- **Marketing (5/5 completed)**: check if social/email content connects to member lifecycle events (onboarding, milestones, churn risk); likely missing referral promotion SOP
- **Facilities (5/6)**: missing supplies/inventory SOP

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO PRIORITIES

## INCREASE REVENUE

- **Pricing is the quickest lever.** AR/M (Average Revenue per Member) has slipped to £120.48 in June vs £165.56 in May — a 27% drop. This isn't member quality; it's package mix. You've grown to 147 active clients but revenue fell £2.5k. Audit what new members are signing up to (introductory tiers? Class packs vs memberships?). Shift new-member onboarding to your full-price tier and upsell June cohort to annual commitment — target AR/M back to £160+.
- **Churn cratered in June (0% is a data error or anomaly) after spiking April–May.** Your 8.33% April attrition, 5.74% May and 6.60% March show retention broke post-relocation. The bigger studio should reduce crowding friction, but it's not working yet. Implement 30-day post-launch check-ins for April–May joiners and offer one free class/month retention credit. Target churn below 2% (November was 1.87%).
- **Capacity utilization is your growth engine now.** The studio is twice the size; June hit 147 members but revenue only £17.7k. In December (124 members), you did £21.1k — that's better yield per head *and* lower churn (0.81%). Launch a "full studio" challenge in Q3: add one peak-time class weekly, introduce a partner/buddy referral bonus (£20 credit per sign-up), and run a corporate wellness trial with 2–3 local firms. Target 180+ active members by September.

## REDUCE EXPENSES

- **Non-people expenses are the red flag.** May £9.2k, June £9.4k on a £20.2k and £17.7k revenue base (45–53% of revenue) vs Sep–Oct baseline of ~35%. April's fit-out is sunk; you should now see non-people settle at £4.5k–£5.5k monthly. June's £9.4k suggests either inflated utilities (new, larger premises), one-off supplier costs, or untracked discretionary spend. Pull the June invoice ledger: flag any non-recurring line items and negotiate utility contracts with a 12-month fixed rate given the bigger footprint.
- **Staffing is proportional and controlled.** People costs June £3.7k on 147 members = £25/member/month. That's healthy.

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **Redistribute Grace's class load immediately.** She's carrying Build: Lower (17 sessions), Elevate (6), Strongevity (3), and No Sweat Intro (3). Assign Build: Lower to JoJo (she's already strong here with 15 sessions); move Elevate and Strongevity to Eilis to build her confidence and spread load; No Sweat Intro can rotate between JoJo and Eilis.

• **JoJo is overloaded at 133 sessions.** She's now running 44 PT slots, 20 InBody scans (which don't count as real coaching), and 6 classes. Cap her PT at 30–35 per month and reduce her class load once Grace's sessions transfer, or she'll burn out as head coach.

• **Eilis is under-used and ready for growth.** She's at 48 sessions with only 11 PT slots and 11 InBody scans (again, not real coaching). Give her 4–6 more PT clients from Grace's or JoJo's overflow, and take on Elevate and Strongevity classes to double her class delivery and build technical range.

• **InBody scans are misclassified.** Grace has done 9, JoJo 20, Eilis 11—these are administrative tasks, not coaching. Slot them into spare minutes or allocate them fairly as admin overhead, don't use them to justify PT load.

• **Create a PT pipeline for Eilis.** She has room for 15–20 PT slots per month. Start her on Grace's lower-risk PT clients and shadow JoJo's complex cases to close the gap safely.

c17ea6e513949b5bd341.text:
**For quiet studio gaps:**

1. **Add a paid "Open Studio" block on Monday/Thursday evenings** (your quietest days) — 60 minutes of unstructured gym access with a coach on-site for form checks. Charges £8-12 per session. Captures members who want flexibility and generates revenue from otherwise empty studio time.

2. **Run a monthly "Barbell Fundamentals" workshop** (Saturday mornings, your quietest day) — 45-minute paid clinic focused on deadlift/squat/bench setup for members intimidated by *Barbelle* class. Position it as a gateway that builds confidence to join the actual class later.

**For quieter classes:**

3. **Move *Engine* to Wednesday or Friday at 6:30am** (your busiest days) — it's a conditioning class that might appeal to members already in a workout mindset on peak attendance days. Pair it with a one-week "try free" invite sent to your *Build* class regulars, since they're your largest engaged segment.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Build for The Forge

**Map your current revenue streams ruthlessly**
- Audit every service you actually deliver: group classes, personal training, nutrition coaching, small-group training, corporate packages, virtual options. List actual prices members pay *now*. You'll spot gaps and pricing inconsistencies immediately.

**Create two separate documents—not one fancy menu**
- **Coaches' version**: Internal pricing, commission structure, package inclusions, upsell triggers, cancellation/refund policies. This stops your team inventing their own rules.
- **Members' version**: Clean, benefits-focused (what results/feeling they get), simple pricing tiers, comparison table showing what "Premium PT" vs "Group Class" includes. Remove internal jargon.

**Price strategically against your positioning**
- You're women-only and community-focused in Belfast—you're *not* competing on budget. Price 15-20% above generic gyms. Research 2-3 premium female-focused gyms in UK. Anchor your pricing there, not below.

**Build in deliberate upsell paths**
- Group class member → Intro to PT (subsidised first session). PT client → Nutrition add-on (+£25/month). Show the progression on your menu so coaches know what to offer when.

**Test with your current members first**
- Share draft menus with 5-10 loyal members before finalizing. They'll tell you what's confusing and what's missing. Use their feedback to refine pricing/bundling within 48 hours.

What revenue stream are you currently under-pricing or not promoting?

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**Insights**

Your cost-per-lead has exploded 350% in three months (£6 → £27), whilst lead volume has collapsed 94% (69 → 4). The close rate is actually holding strong at 75%, so the problem isn't conversion—it's reach and relevance decay. July's spend floor (£109) suggests you either pulled budget or hit severe ad fatigue. Your April performance (£6/lead, 20 sales on £435) is the benchmark you've lost, not a fluke.

The trajectory is a textbook creative and audience exhaustion curve: initial cold-audience novelty wore off, you're now retargeting the same women repeatedly without fresh creative, and Meta's algorithm has stopped finding lookalikes at scale. The 75% close rate proves your offer and positioning work—you're just not reaching new prospects efficiently anymore.

**Actions**

1. **Immediately rebuild creative pipeline.** You're likely running the same 1–2 video or image ads since April. Test three new angles this week: (a) transformation story (before/after testimony from a member, 15–20 sec video), (b) social proof (group energy/community moment in the gym, less about fitness, more about belonging), (c) objection-led ("6 weeks is enough—here's why"). Each needs fresh footage. Pause the exhausted creatives.

2. **Segment your audience—stop spraying.** Instead of one broad "women 25–45 interested in fitness," split into: cold lookalikes (0% website visitors, new), warm website visitors (past 180 days, non-converters), and engaged (video viewers, add-to-cart, over 3 sec video engagement). Run £30–40/week to cold lookalikes *only* with new creative. The exhaustion is happening in your lookalike set; fresh creatives + smaller, fresh audience pools will drop CPL back to £8–12.

3. **Reset your budget tactically.** July at £109 spend won't drive volume. Next month, commit £250–300 minimum split: £100–120 to cold lookalike testing (new creative), £80–100 to warm retargeting (existing site visitors

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ACTIONS

## INCREASE REVENUE
- **Fix the member-value cliff.** Rev/member has dropped from £138 (April) → £116–£109 (May–June). At 145 members, you're leaving £27–29k annually on the table. Audit class pricing (are peak classes undersold?), introduce tiered membership tiers (premium peak-hour, budget off-peak), and push add-ons: PT packages, nutrition plans, apparel. Target: £130+ rev/member within 8 weeks.
- **Arrest churn spikes.** December (11.8%) and May (11.9%) are outliers; Jan–June average is 6.3%. Root cause: post-holiday drop-off or onboarding failure? Run exit surveys, tighten first-month engagement (buddy system, week-1 check-in) and lock 6-month contracts at sign-up rather than monthly rolling. This alone could add 8–12 retained members.
- **Fill quiet slots systematically.** You have 145 members; if average class capacity is 15–20, you're not selling out all hours. Offer "off-peak passes" (£30–40/month for 06:00–10:00 and 14:00–16:00 slots) to shift demand and lower peak-hour overcrowding complaints.

## REDUCE EXPENSES
- **March and June cash-outs are abnormally high** (£23.3k and £20.6k on £14–16k revenue). Excluding the April move, these months are burning 140–130% of monthly revenue. Get a line-by-line breakdown: are supplier terms slipping (paying invoices late-month that should be spread)? Are staff bonuses or stock purchases embedded here? Tighten to 85–90% of monthly revenue within 60 days.
- **Review staffing cost-to-revenue ratio.** Typical gym labour is 35–45% of revenue; you're running 120–140% cash out vs revenue most months (excluding April). Either payroll is oversized for 145 members, or non-payroll spend (rent, utilities, stock) is high. Segregate payroll from fixed costs and report both. If payroll >50% of revenue, defer hires or shift to part-time instructor pool.

## IMPROVE PROFITABILITY & TAX
- **Corporation tax and salary/dividend split.** At £15–17k monthly revenue (annualised ~£190k), you're likely profitable post-expenses but the bank account tells a different story.
